title: Divine Imagination and the Intermediate World: Ibn Arabi on the Barzakh
author: James W. Morris

body.text: James W. Morris introduces a concept from Ibn Arabi that is at once simple and bewildering: the Barzakh. The word literally means 'barrier' or 'isthmus' in Arabic. Ibn Arabi uses it to name the intermediate realm between the sensory world and the intelligible world. But this is not merely a place. It is the divine Imagination itself, the ontological faculty through which all spiritual perception becomes possible.

For centuries after the time of the Andalusian mystic Ibn Arabi, his remarkable discussions of the Imagination were elaborated by Islamic philosophers, poets, artists and critics in order to explain, interpret and justify the full range of artistic and creative activities carried on within later Islamicate cultures, as far away as India and Indonesia. Another major source of those classical Islamic understandings of the Imagination was in the Shaykh's many discussions of the eschatological Barzakh or intermediate world of the divine Imagination, as well as his accounts of his own striking experiences and decisive spiritual encounters there, which are scattered throughout his magnum opus, al-Futuhat al-Makkiya.

Morris warns the reader immediately: Ibn Arabi is not trying to clarify in a rational, conceptual sense. His writing aims to transform the reader. The constant shifts between perspectives, images, and technical vocabularies are deliberate. They break down the ordinary dualities of subject and object, divine and human, spiritual and material.

Even the most cursory reading of this chapter would make quickly evident that Ibn Arabi's intention was not to clarify in any sort of rational, conceptual form the different ways we can speak of the Imagination. The recurrent mixture of and sudden shifts between a whole spectrum of contrasting approaches, images, perspectives and technical vocabularies is not accidental but instead forms a complex, highly self-conscious rhetorical technique. His ultimate aim is not simply heightened intellectual understanding, but to bring about in the properly prepared reader a suddenly transformed state of immediate realization in which each of the implicit dualities of our usual perception of things is directly transcended in an enlightened moment of unitive vision. His aim is to bring his readers to see everything as ongoing theophany and ever-renewed divine creation.

Ibn Arabi's own definition of the Barzakh is deceptively simple. It is what separates two things without ever becoming either of them. A line separating shadow from sunlight. The divider between two seas that keeps them from mixing. But the intellect judges that there is something there, even when the senses cannot perceive it. That something, grasped only by the intellect, is the Barzakh.

Know that the word 'barzakh' is an expression for what separates two things without ever becoming either of them, such as the line separating a shadow from the sunlight. Even if our senses are unable to perceive what separates those two things, the intellect judges that there is indeed a divider separating them, and that divider grasped by the intellect is precisely the barzakh. Because if something is perceived by the senses, it must be one of those two things, rather than the barzakh. Each of those two things, when they are adjacent to each other, have need of a barzakh which is not the same as each of them, but which has in itself the power of each of them.

Here Ibn Arabi makes the crucial move: the Barzakh is not a passive divider. It has the power of both sides. It participates in both realities while being reducible to neither. This makes it the perfect description of the Imagination itself, which partakes of both the spiritual and the material, the real and the apparent, the known and the unknown.

Now since the Barzakh of the creative divine Imagination is something separating what is knowable and unknowable, existent and non-existent, intelligible and unintelligible, affirmed and negated, it has been given the name Barzakh as a technical term. It is intelligible in itself, yet it is nothing but the imagined-image. For when you perceive it, assuming you are in a rational state, you know that you have perceived something existent on which your gaze has fallen. You most definitely know that there is absolutely something there. But what is this about which you affirm that it is an existent thing, while at the same time you are also denying that? For this Imagination-Image is neither entirely existent nor non-existent, neither entirely known nor unknowable, neither entirely affirmed nor denied.

Ibn Arabi gives the example of seeing your reflection in a mirror. You know you have perceived your form, yet you also know that the form you see is not really you. It is smaller than you. It exists in a different way. The reflected form is both affirmed and denied, both existent and non-existent. If you cannot fully grasp the reality of this simple phenomenon, how much less can you grasp the reality of the divine self-manifestations that come through the same imaginal mode?

This is like a human being perceiving their reflected form in the mirror. The person definitely knows that they have perceived their own form in a certain respect, while they know just as absolutely that they have not perceived their form in another respect, because of the smallness of the image they see. What is that reflected form? And where is it actually located? And what is its ontological status? For it is both affirmed and denied, both existent and non-existent, both known and unknown. Now God has made this reality appear to His servants so that they might know that if they are bewildered and incapable of grasping the reality of this phenomenon which is only part of this world, then how much more incapable they must be regarding the Creator of that reality. In this way God has pointed out that the divine Self-manifestations to them are even more subtle and delicate than this case in which their intellects are already so bewildered.

Ibn Arabi distinguishes between two modes of perception: the eye of physical sensation and the eye of imagination. The same object can be perceived through either. The test for which eye is active is whether the perceived object changes form when observed. If a chameleon changes color as you watch it, and the fact of your watching does not stop it from changing, then you are seeing with the eye of imagination, not the eye of sensation. The imagination does not freeze its object. It participates in its流动.

There are some people who perceive this imaged-object with the eye of physical sensation, and there are others who perceive it with the eye of imagination. If a person wishes to distinguish between those two modes of perception in their waking state, they can determine it by looking at the imaged-object. Thus if the states of what one is looking at continue to change as it changes its shape and characteristics, even though you cannot deny that it is still the same thing, and the fact of observing it does not stop it from changing its shape and formation, as when a chameleon when you observe it stops changing its colors, then that is undoubtedly being seen with the eye of imagination, not with the eye of the senses.

This distinction is crucial for understanding spiritual vision. When people claim to see spirits, angels, or even God in a dream, they are seeing with the eye of imagination. This does not make the vision unreal. It makes it real in the mode of the Barzakh. The judgment about what the vision means belongs to the intellect, not to the imagination. The imagination itself is never false. Only the interpretation can be mistaken.

Few of those who lay claim to the unveiled vision of spirits really understand how this is. When those spirits take on the image of perceptible forms, most people do not know whether they are perceiving them with the eye of the imagination or with the eye of physical sensation. This is how you should understand how a human being can perceive their Lord in a dream, even though He transcends any form or image. So you should know with which eye you are seeing Him! For I have already let you know that the divine Imagination is perceived either through itself, through the eye of imagination, or through physical vision.

Ibn Arabi develops the eschatological dimension through the image of the Horn. The Arabic word al-sur means both 'horn' and is the plural of 'form.' The Horn that the angel Israfil blows at the Resurrection is simultaneously the realm of forms in which souls exist after death. It is made of light, because light is what makes things visible. This Horn is the Barzakh itself.

The prescriber of Revelation called this thing, which is the divine Presence of the Barzakh to which we are brought after death and in which we directly witness our souls, a Horn and Trumpet. Here the word al-sur is also the plural of the word sura, 'form.' Know that the Messenger of God, when he was asked what this Horn was, replied: 'It is a horn of Light upon which the angel Israfil blows.' The breadth of this Horn of the Barzakh-Imagination is as broad as possible, since none of the generated things are broader than It. That is because through Its reality It has sway over every thing, and even over what is not a thing. It gives form alike to absolute nothingness and to what is ontologically impossible, necessary and contingent, turning what is existent into nothingness and what is non-existent into being.

The shape of the Horn is paradoxical: the narrow end is at the top, the wide end at the bottom. This reverses ordinary expectation. It means that the highest spiritual realities, the divine Essence, are the most concentrated, the most narrow. The lower one descends toward multiplicity, the wider the realm becomes. The path of spiritual knowledge is a movement from width to narrowness, from many objects of knowledge to the One.

Since the Imagination gives form to God as well as everything in the world below Him, its highest section is the narrow one and its lowest section is the wide one. That is just how God created It: for the first thing He created from It was narrow, while what He created from It later widened out. When the true Knowers want to proceed to knowing the Unicity of God, they keep on ascending gradually from that breadth toward what is narrower. Their forms of knowing keep on becoming less numerous while they ascend in their knowledge of Gods Essence, until they reach the point where they have no object of knowledge but God alone, which is the narrowest point in that Horn.

After death, spirits are deposited in imaginal-bodily forms within this Horn of Light. They perceive through the eye and light of the form they inhabit. This is why dreams are always true: the dream itself is never false, only the interpretation can be mistaken. Ibn Arabi illustrates this with the Prophet's response to a man who dreamed his head was cut off and continued speaking: the Prophet did not say 'your imagination is false.' He knew what the man saw was real, but the interpretation was wrong.

When God takes the spirits from these physical bodies wherever they may be at death, He deposits those spirits in imaginal-bodily forms within this Horn of Light. All of those things that the human being perceives after death in the Barzakh are only perceived through the eye and with the light of the form in which that person exists in that Horn, and that is a true perception. This is why one's dreams are always true, because every dream in itself is true and not in error. Because if we say a dream was mistaken, it is not really the dream itself that was mistaken, but rather the person who interpreted it incorrectly, in that they did not recognize the intended meaning of the image in question.

Morris concludes with the most remarkable claim of all: there is no false imagination. The imagination perceives exactly what it perceives, without judgment. Error belongs only to the intellect's interpretation. This means that every image, every vision, every dream is real in the mode of the Barzakh. The spiritual task is not to distinguish real from false images, but to learn to interpret correctly what is already real.

As for our companions, they were mistaken in their thinking about this Horn. They considered the wide segment of this Horn its highest part and its narrow section the lowest part of the world, but things are not at all as they claimed. The imagination perceives with its light whatever it perceives, without passing judgment. The judgment only belongs to something else, which is the intellect, so the error cannot be attributed to the imagination. Thus there never is any false imagination at all, indeed all of it is correct. Every human being in the Barzakh is hostage to what they have acquired, imprisoned in the forms of their deeds, until they are raised up from those forms on the Day of the Rising.
